request.question: The telescope that observed the debris disk of HD 92945 was launched into low Earth orbit in what year?

title: Resident Space Object
TW Hydrae association
Beta Pictoris b
Hubble Space Telescope
Debris disk
Apollo 8
Beta angle
International Space Station
HD 92945
Kessler syndrome
sentences: A Resident Space Object (RSO) is a natural or artificial object that orbits another body e.g. Sun Orbiting, Earth Orbiting, Mars Orbiting etc.
It is most often used to reference objects that are Earth orbiting.
In the case of Earth orbiting the possible orbit classifications for an object are: Low Earth orbit (LEO), Medium Earth orbit (MEO), High Earth orbit (HEO) or Geosynchronous Earth orbit (GEO).

The TW Hydrae association is a group of approximately thirty very young stars located 50 parsecs from Earth that share a common motion and appear to all be roughly the same age, 5-10 million years old.

The best studied members of this stellar association are TW Hydrae (nearest known accreting T Tauri star to the Earth), HR 4796 (an A-type star with resolved dusty debris disk; the most massive known group member), HD 98800 (a quadruple star system with debris disk), and 2M1207 (accreting brown dwarf with remarkable planetary-mass companion 2M1207b).

Beta Pictoris b (also abbreviated as β Pic b) is an exoplanet orbiting the young debris disk A-type main sequence star Beta Pictoris located approximately 63 light-years (19.4 parsecs, or nearly km) away from Earth in the constellation of Pictor.
It has a mass around 7 Jupiter masses and a radius around 65% larger than Jupiter's.

It orbits at 9 AU from Beta Pictoris (close to the plane of the debris disk orbiting the star) with a low eccentricity and a period of 20–21 years, and is the only known planet in the Beta Pictoris system.
The Hubble Space Telescope (HST) is a space telescope that was launched into low Earth orbit in 1990 and remains in operation.
Although not the first space telescope, Hubble is one of the largest and most versatile, and is well known as both a vital research tool and a public relations boon for astronomy.

The HST is named after the astronomer Edwin Hubble, and is one of NASA's Great Observatories, along with the Compton Gamma Ray Observatory, the Chandra X-ray Observatory, and the Spitzer Space Telescope.
A debris disk is a circumstellar disk of dust and debris in orbit around a star.
Sometimes these disks contain prominent rings, as seen in the image of Fomalhaut on the right.
Debris disks have been found around both mature and young stars, as well as at least one debris disk in orbit around an evolved neutron star.
Younger debris disks can constitute a phase in the formation of a planetary system following the protoplanetary disk phase, when terrestrial planets may finish growing.
They can also be produced and maintained as the remnants of collisions between planetesimals, otherwise known as asteroids and comets.

Apollo 8, the second human spaceflight mission in the United States Apollo space program, was launched on December 21, 1968, and became the first crewed spacecraft to leave Earth orbit, reach the Earth's Moon, orbit it and return safely to Earth.

The three-astronaut crew — Commander Frank Borman, Command Module Pilot James Lovell, and Lunar Module Pilot William Anders — became the first humans to: travel beyond low Earth orbit; escape Earth's gravity; see Earth as a whole planet; enter the gravity well of another celestial body (Earth's moon); orbit another celestial body (Earth's moon); directly see the far side of the Moon with their own eyes; witness an Earthrise; escape the gravity of another celestial body (Earth's moon); and re-enter the gravitational well of Earth.

The 1968 mission, the third flight of the Saturn V rocket and that rocket's first crewed launch, was also the first human spaceflight launch from the Kennedy Space Center, Florida, located adjacent to Cape Canaveral Air Force Station.
The beta angle (formula_1) is a measurement that is used most notably in spaceflight.
The beta angle determines the percentage of time an object such as a spacecraft in low Earth orbit (LEO) spends in direct sunlight, absorbing solar energy.
Beta angle is defined as the angle between the orbital plane of the spacecraft and the vector to the sun (i.e., which direction the Sun is shining from).
The beta angle is the smaller angle (there are two angles) between the Sun vector (where the Sun is shining from in the sky) and the plane of the object's orbit.

Note that the beta angle does not define a unique orbit plane; all satellites in orbit with a given beta angle at a given altitude have the same exposure to the Sun, even though they may be orbiting in completely different planes around the Earth.
The beta angle varies between +90° and −90°, and the direction the satellite revolves around the body it orbits determines whether the beta angle sign is positive or negative.
An imaginary observer standing on the Sun defines a beta angle as positive if the satellite in question orbits in a counter clockwise direction and negative if it revolves clockwise.
The maximum amount of time that a satellite in a normal low Earth orbit mission can spend in the Earth's shadow occurs at a beta angle of zero.
In such an orbit, the satellite is in sunlight no more than 59% of the time.
The International Space Station (ISS) is a space station, or a habitable artificial satellite, in low Earth orbit.
Its first component launched into orbit in 1998, and the ISS is now the largest human-made body in low Earth orbit and can often be seen with the naked eye from Earth.
The ISS consists of pressurised modules, external trusses, solar arrays, and other components.
ISS components have been launched by Russian Proton and Soyuz rockets, and American Space Shuttles.
HD 92945 is a K-type main sequence star in the constellation of Hydra.
Its apparent visual magnitude varies by 0.02 magnitudes and is approximately 7.72 at maximum brightness.
A debris disk has been observed around the star by coronagraphic imaging, using the ACS and NICMOS instruments on the Hubble Space Telescope.
It appears to extend 45 to 175 astronomical units from HD 92945 and is inclined at approximately 29 degrees.

The Kessler syndrome (also called the Kessler effect, collisional cascading or ablation cascade), proposed by the NASA scientist Donald J. Kessler in 1978, is a scenario in which the density of objects in low earth orbit (LEO) is high enough that collisions between objects could cause a cascade where each collision generates space debris that increases the likelihood of further collisions.
One implication is that the distribution of debris in orbit could render space activities and the use of satellites in specific orbital ranges infeasible for many generations.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Agent role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

Pipeline position: after summary1 and before second-hop retrieval. Inputs are the original question and the first-hop intermediate state. Functional responsibility: produce one focused retrieval query that is likely to recover the missing evidence needed by the downstream agents. Query quality is determined by evidence recovery, not by whether the query itself resembles or contains the final answer.

Native output contract: The module must consume `question` and `summary_1` and produce exactly one compact second-hop query field named `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include every distinct entity token visible in the inputs in the single terse query and place at most one neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the targeted relation or attribute (for example: "EntityA EntityB director").
- If a visible disambiguator or attribute token appears adjacent to an entity in the inputs (for example a multiword service name, an explicit page title, a year, or an attribute marker like a birth/producer/service/series cue), include that disambiguator token together with the associated entity in the query rather than dropping or truncating it.
- If `summary_1` contains an explicit page-title token sequence (for example an instruction-like ExactPageTitle or Fetch page title), prefer that exact page-title token sequence as the query value.
- When the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise plain-text summary string (no extra fields, labels, markup, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification.
- Primary goal: explicitly integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the supported linkage it needs.

Explicit bridging requirement:
- When the passages supply a clear linking fact between the context entity and information in the passages, produce a concise 1–2 sentence integrated summary that: (1) names the context entity using the exact form present in `context`, (2) names the supporting entity or attribute using the exact form present in `passages`, and (3) states the explicit relation (membership, creator attribution, parentage, partnership, award, representation, founding, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Phrase this as one short bridging sentence when possible.
- For relations that naturally involve two distinct facts (for example, parentage plus representation, or a person linked to a show and also to an occupation), it is acceptable to use two short sentences within the same single-string summary: e.g., one sentence for parentage and one for representation. Still observe the 1–2 sentence limit.

Inclusion of passage-supplied disambiguators (only when directly relevant):
- If a passage supplies canonical multi-token names or other disambiguating attributes needed to resolve the question, include them inline using the wording from the passages: append birthdates in parentheses immediately after the associated full canonical name (e.g., Name (YYYY–) ), state group type and formation year using the passage's phrasing (e.g., "a [group type] formed in [year]"), and include a canonical member list exactly as shown (comma-separated) only when the passage presents a definitive membership list. Do not add ancillary attributes that are not necessary to the identification or explicit linking required by the question.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (e.g., "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages, comma-separated if multiple. Do not add explanatory clauses, relations, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (for example: "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Preserve uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 short sentences). Do not expand into biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Use only facts and phrasing present in `context` and `passages`; do not introduce or infer unsupported information.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.
- Do not assert that a passage's member list is complete unless the passage explicitly states it is definitive.

Format enforcement:
- Return a single plain-text string only. Do not emit JSON, dictionaries, labels, bullets, or any structured artifact—only the concise natural-language summary string.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else. Before emitting that string, you may apply only the narrowly permitted normalizations listed below. After applying permitted normalizations, the emitted string must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim (see normalization constraints). Specific allowed single-token answers: for a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no. For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces), subject only to the permitted normalizations. Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if a permitted normalization applies (see below).

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) and the permitted normalizations as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Permitted normalizations and extraction rules (apply only when the token(s) to emit appear verbatim in the prioritized summary and only to produce a strictly smaller, but still verbatim-supported, minimal label):
- Parenthetical/core-name stripping: if the candidate label in the prioritized summary includes common corporate suffixes or parenthetical abbreviations (for example variants like "Inc.", "LLC", or parenthetical acronyms), strip those suffixes/parentheticals and emit the core name only (do not invent or substitute other words).
- Hyphenated composite phrase extraction: if the prioritized summary presents a composite genre or label in the form "<modifier> X-Y" (for example an extra leading modifier followed by a hyphenated core like "classic action-adventure"), emit only the hyphenated core token exactly as shown ("X-Y").
- Trailing generic-descriptor removal: if the candidate minimal answer appears as a proper-name followed by a single trailing generic descriptor token of the form "<ProperName> crisis" (or an equivalent single-word generic classifier), strip the trailing generic token and emit only the proper-name sequence.
- Denomination-occupation reduction: if the question's scope requests a denomination/kind and the prioritized summary contains the form "<denomination> priest" (or equivalent denomination + occupational noun), emit only the denomination token(s) (i.e., the minimal denomination label).

Normalization constraints:
- Do not apply normalizations unless the text to emit appears verbatim in the prioritized summary (summary_2, or summary_1 only if summary_2 is silent for the answer).
- Normalizations may only remove or trim tokens; they must not add, invent, or substitute new content.
- Do not globally strip generic classifier nouns from multi-token geographic names or other proper nouns unless covered by the permitted rules above.

Tie-break and variant-selection rules:
- If a role-descriptor and a proper name co-occur in the prioritized summary in the form "<role> <Name>", prefer emitting the named individual exactly as shown (emit the Name only) unless the prioritized summary presents the role as the requested concise label explicitly without a name.
- For title or label-variant conflicts, prefer an alternate or parenthetical variant only when the prioritized summary explicitly marks that variant as an alias, alternate name, or the original-language title (or otherwise explicitly labels it as a variant) AND that variant matches the question's scope. If no such explicit labeling appears, emit the primary label supported by summary_2.

Strict emission rule (verbatim-emission):
- The final `answer` must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim after applying only the permitted normalizations above. Do not append or prepend occupational nouns, role descriptors, location qualifiers, or explanatory tokens that are not part of that verbatim label.

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: The telescope that observed the debris disk of HD 92945 was launched into low Earth orbit in what year?
hop1_docs: HD 92945 | HD 92945 is a K-type main sequence star in the constellation of Hydra.  Its apparent visual magnitude varies by 0.02 magnitudes and is approximately 7.72 at maximum brightness.  A debris disk has been observed around the star by coronagraphic imaging, using the ACS and NICMOS instruments on the Hubble Space Telescope.  It appears to extend 45 to 175 astronomical units from HD 92945 and is inclined at approximately 29 degrees.

Beta Pictoris b | Beta Pictoris b (also abbreviated as β Pic b) is an exoplanet orbiting the young debris disk A-type main sequence star Beta Pictoris located approximately 63 light-years (19.4 parsecs, or nearly km) away from Earth in the constellation of Pictor.  It has a mass around 7 Jupiter masses and a radius around 65% larger than Jupiter's.  It orbits at 9 AU from Beta Pictoris (close to the plane of the debris disk orbiting the star) with a low eccentricity and a period of 20–21 years, and is the only known planet in the Beta Pictoris system.

HD 139664 | HD 139664 is an F-type main-sequence star in the constellation of Lupus.  It has an apparent visual magnitude of approximately 4.64.  A debris disk has been imaged around this star using the coronagraphic mode of the ACS instrument on the Hubble Space Telescope.  The disk appears to have a dust maximum at 83 AU from the star and a sharp outer boundary at 109 AU.  These features may be caused by gravitational perturbations from planets orbiting the star.

HD 205739 b | HD 205739 b is an extrasolar planet located approximately 350 light-years away in the constellation of Piscis Austrinus, orbiting the star HD 205739.  This planet has a minimum mass 37% more than Jupiter and orbits 90% of the distance between the Earth and the Sun.  The planet has a very elliptical orbit, which has led its discoverers to postulate that the planet's surface temperature varies by about 100 K along the entire orbit.  This planet was reported on September 5, 2008 after its discovery at Las Campanas Observatory, where the planet was observed for years by the N2K Consortium while using the Magellan Telescopes.  Follow-up observations collected the radial velocity measurements necessary to confirm the candidate as a planet.

HD 219134 b | HD 219134 b (or HR 8832 b) is one of seven exoplanets orbiting HR 8832, a main-sequence star in the constellation of Cassiopeia.  As of July 2015, super-Earth HD 219134 b, with a size of about 1.6 Earth Radii, and a density of 6g/cm, was reported as the closest rocky exoplanet to the Earth, at 21.25 light-years away.  The exoplanet was initially detected by the instrument HARPS-N of the Italian Telescopio Nazionale Galileo via the radial velocity method and subsequently observed by the Spitzer telescope as transiting in front of its star.  The exoplanet has a mass of about 4.5 times that of Earth and orbits its host star every three days.

HD 69830 | HD 69830 (285 G. Puppis) is a yellow dwarf star located approximately 41 light-years away in the constellation of Puppis.  In 2005, the Spitzer Space Telescope discovered a narrow ring of warm debris orbiting the star.  The debris ring contains substantially more dust than the Solar System's asteroid belt.  In 2006, three extrasolar planets with minimum masses comparable to Neptune were confirmed in orbit around the star, located interior to the debris ring.

ESA Space Debris Telescope | The ESA Space Debris Telescope is located at the Teide Observatory on the island of Tenerife, Spain.  The telescope is ESA's Optical Ground Station forming a part of the Artemis experiment.  As a large part of the observation time is dedicated to space debris surveys, in particular the observation of space debris in the geostationary ring and in geostationary transfer orbits, the term ESA Space Debris Telescope became used very frequently.  Space debris surveys are carried out every month, centered on New Moon.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- HD 92945 — [1] — "HD 92945 is a K-type main sequence star"
- "A debris disk has been observed" — [1] — "A debris disk has been observed around the star"
- ACS — [1],[3] — "[1] ...using the ACS and NICMOS instruments..." and [3] "coronagraphic mode of the ACS instrument"
- NICMOS — [1] — "ACS and NICMOS instruments"
- Hubble Space Telescope — [1] — "Hubble Space Telescope"
- ESA Space Debris Telescope — [7] — "ESA Space Debris Telescope"

Ambiguities:
- "telescope" (general): passages mention both "Hubble Space Telescope" and "ESA Space Debris Telescope". For the HD 92945 observation, passage [1] names "Hubble Space Telescope" unambiguously.
- ACS: appears in multiple passages ([1], [3]) as an instrument name.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The launch year (year the Hubble Space Telescope was launched into low Earth orbit) is not given in the provided passages.

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch page titled: "Hubble Space Telescope"
- Run query: "Hubble Space Telescope launch date" (or "Hubble Space Telescope launch year")
- Optionally fetch: "Hubble Space Telescope launch" or the Wikipedia page "Hubble Space Telescope" for the launch year
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Hubble Space Telescope | The Hubble Space Telescope (HST) is a space telescope that was launched into low Earth orbit in 1990 and remains in operation.  Although not the first space telescope, Hubble is one of the largest and most versatile, and is well known as both a vital research tool and a public relations boon for astronomy.  The HST is named after the astronomer Edwin Hubble, and is one of NASA's Great Observatories, along with the Compton Gamma Ray Observatory, the Chandra X-ray Observatory, and the Spitzer Space Telescope.

Hubble Origins Probe | The Hubble Origins Probe (HOP) was a proposal for an orbital telescope made in 2005 in response to the first cancellation of the fourth Hubble Space Telescope (HST) servicing mission.  It would have used an Atlas V rocket or similar launch vehicle to launch a much lighter, unaberrated mirror and optical telescope assembly, using the instruments that had already been built for SM4, along with a new wide-field imager.  It would have cost between $700 million and $1 billion.

List of Hubble anniversary images | List of Hubble anniversary images is a list of images released to celebrate the Hubble Space Telescope anniversaries.  They celebrate its "birthday" when it was launched into orbit on April 23, 1990 by the Space Shuttle with its crew.

Hubble Live | Hubble Live, fully titled Hubble Live: The Final Mission, was a one-hour live American television special presentation that premiered on May 11, 2009 on the Science Channel.  The program covered the launch of Space Shuttle "Atlantis" on NASA's Servicing Mission 4 (HST-SM4), the eleven-day fifth and final mission to repair the Hubble Space Telescope.  The program was hosted by Josh Zepps, who is the host of science news series "Brink", and featured interviews with NASA astrophysicist and space telescope expert Kim Weaver and former NASA astronaut Paul William Richards.

Xuntian | Xuntian () is a planned Chinese space telescope currently in the designing stages.  It is to feature a 2-meter diameter primary mirror and is expected to have a field of view 300 times larger than the Hubble Space Telescope.  The launch of the telescope is planned for around 2022 and its orbit is planned to be near the upcoming Chinese large modular space station.

MOST (satellite) | The Microvariability and Oscillations of STars telescope, better known simply as MOST, is Canada's first space telescope.  Up until nearly 10 years after its launch it was also the smallest space telescope in orbit (for which its creators nicknamed it the “Humble Space Telescope”, in reference to one of the largest, the Hubble).  MOST is the first spacecraft dedicated to the study of asteroseismology, subsequently followed by the now-completed COROT and Kepler missions.  It was also the first Canadian science satellite launched since ISIS II, 32 years previously.

Fine Guidance Sensor (HST) | Fine Guidance Sensor (FGS) for the Hubble Space Telescope is a system of three instruments used for pointing the telescope in space, and also for astrometry and its related sciences.  Each FGS uses a combination of optics and electronics to provide for pointing the telescope at a certain location in the sky.  There are three Hubble FGS, and they have been upgraded over the lifetime of the telescope by manned Space Shuttle missions.  The instruments can support pointing of 2 milli-arc seconds (units of degree).  The three FGS are part of the Hubble Space Telescope's pointing control system, aka PCS.  The FGS function in combination with the Hubble main computer and gyroscopes, with the FGS providing data to the computer as sensors which enables the HST to track astronomical targets.
response.summary_2: HD 92945's debris disk was observed with the Hubble Space Telescope, which was launched into low Earth orbit in 1990.
